story:
The lights have hummed at B-flat since Monday. I keep track. Patterns hold me together. At my desk I push numbers my boss pretends to understand. Miller drops a paper on my files. RIPOSTE DOES IT AGAIN – SAVES CHILD THOUGHT DEAD. I do the office face. Brows up. Small nod. Inside, I correct them. Wrong street. Missing fifteen minutes with the man in the green coat. No fire escape mention. That’s where I caught her hand. Where I almost didn’t.

He taps the grainy photo. “Guy’s a ghost.” I smile. I know exactly how much the cops hate me.

Five o’clock scrapes across the floor. People migrate. Tea, traffic, weekend. I stack reports, straighten pens, shut down. Walk out like I belong to the carpet. Mask on: even shoulders, casual stride, polite mouth. Elevator doors close and hand me a reflection of Anyone. Outside, exhaust and wet pavement. I file the city by rhythm and wrong notes. You can’t look like you’re watching.

Home. Three clicks. Quiet drops into place. Overheads stay dark; they’re too loud. Shoes off. Tie second. The chair by the window knows me. Knees up, rock forward, back, forward, back until the static loosens. The repetition is the point. The control. “Stimming,” some say. Doctors say coping. Autism and ADHD on paper. AuDHD as shorthand. Disability if you want a box. For me, it’s the operating system. Always running. Always processing. No off switch. You learn the noise. You learn the weather of your own head.

Jaw unclenches. Microwaved rice waits ninety seconds; I press my palms to my eyes for relief, not art. No one sees this part. Adam Brick, neat analyst. Riposte, rumor in the alleys. Neither exists without the shutdown and the climb back.

Three monitors wake. Corkboard above, string cutting the city into pieces only I recognize. Notes in inks you can’t find twice. People think I hunt monsters for them. I don’t. I hunt patterns because I can’t stand the splinters. Hyperfocus sounds like a gift. It isn’t. It’s an itch inside the bone. It stops when the answer lands.

A ping. Missing person. Male, forty-two. Last seen near the harbor. Police: probable runaway, no foul play. Translation: not worth overtime. I scroll. There it is—the off-beat. Witness says he left at 7:15. Lobby camera shows 7:19. Different shoes. Shallow tread on a wet night. No reflection in the glass where there should be one. My skin pricks. The hook sets. The noise starts to line up.

Dinner cools to nothing. The file widens on the center screen. Oxygen. I stand. Closet in the dark. Coat, canvas and quiet. Boots worn to silence. Gloves with grip and no prints. Check, check, check. The mask last. Not for fear. For erasing edges. Cameras guess twice. People look away.

Some say a mask turns you into someone else. They’re wrong. It lets you stop pretending. By the time the coat settles, the office version is gone. The city is a score of hums and wrong notes, and I’ve found the one that doesn’t belong. Only the question remains. Only the answer I have to pull out. Only Riposte.
